description: Linguistic reconstruction proposing Dravidian loanwords and structural evidence for locating the earliest Indo-European homeland in South Asia.

title: Antecedents Of Rig Vedic Religion
description: Traces Central Asian and Hindukush cultural influences on early Vedic religious practices, mapping ritual and mythological developments.

title: Excavating Vedic Harappans
description: Synthesizes archaeological findings demonstrating cultural continuity between Indus Valley settlements and early Vedic material culture.

title: Coexistence of Culture and Civilization ISC and OCP
description: Analyzes the coexistence and interaction between Indus Valley Civilization and Ganga Plain Chalcolithic cultures, highlighting trade and cultural exchange.

title: Animal Subsistence In Ganga Valley
description: Zooarchaeological study of animal husbandry and hunting practices in the Ganga Valley, tracing subsistence strategies from prehistoric to early historic periods.

title: Eurasian Spreadzone And IE Dispersal
description: Linguistic typology study on Eurasian language spread zones, modeling mechanisms behind Indo-European linguistic expansion across continents.

title: Ancient Cattle Genomics
description: Genomic analysis of ancient cattle domestication in South Asia, revealing independent taurine and indicine lineages and their historical dispersal.

title: Epicenter of the Indo-European Linguistic Spread
description: Identifies potential linguistic epicenters for Indo-European expansion, evaluating geographic, demographic, and typological factors in language dispersal.

title: Chronology And Culture History In The Indus Valley
description: Comprehensive chronological framework for Indus Valley cultural phases, integrating stratigraphy, artifact typology, and regional settlement patterns.

title: AIT And Linguistics
description: Critical evaluation of linguistic arguments used to support the Aryan Invasion Theory, highlighting methodological assumptions and alternative models.

title: Discovering The Sarasvati River
description: Historical overview of Sarasvati River research from 19th-century explorations to modern hydrological and archaeological discoveries.

title: Chera Chola Pandya Using Archaeological Evidence to Identify the Tamil Kingdoms of Early Historic South India
description: Archaeological synthesis identifying material culture, trade networks, and political structures of early Chera, Chola, and Pandya kingdoms.

title: Absence Of Sword From Rig Veda
description: Philological and archaeological analysis of the missing sword terminology in the Rig Veda, discussing implications for Indo-Aryan chronology.

title: Demilitarizing The Rigveda
description: Re-evaluates Vedic texts to contextualize references to horses, chariots, and warfare, challenging militarized interpretations of early Aryan culture.

title: Autochthonous Aryans
description: Academic critique of indigenous Aryan theories, contrasting textual and linguistic evidence with mainstream Indo-European migration models.

title: A Timeline Of Ayodhya
description: Historical and archaeological timeline of Ayodhya, tracing settlement layers, textual references, and cultural continuity from ancient to medieval periods.

title: Decipherment Of Indus Valley Vedic Seals
description: Proposes Vedic linguistic correlations for Indus Valley seal inscriptions, integrating symbolic analysis with early Sanskrit vocabulary.

title: Asura And Its Derivatives In Rigveda
description: Philological study of 'Asura' and related terms in the Rig Veda, tracing semantic shifts from divine epithets to later mythological antagonists.

title: A Prehistoric Thoroughfare Between The Ganges and the Himalaya
description: Linguistic and archaeological mapping of ancient trade and migration routes connecting the Ganga basin to Himalayan foothills.

title: From Sarasvati To Ganga
description: Traces the cultural and geographical shift from Sarasvati-centered Vedic civilization to Ganga-dominated historical India, analyzing hydrological changes.

title: Decipherment Of Indus Valley Seals
description: Proposes a rebus-based decipherment model for Indus script, linking iconography to early Dravidian and Sanskrit linguistic structures.

title: Astronomical Chronology Of Vedic Literature
description: Uses archaeoastronomy to date Vedic texts based on celestial references, proposing earlier chronologies for Rigvedic and post-Vedic compositions.

title: A Note On PIE And Nuclear Nostratic
description: Preliminary linguistic analysis exploring potential genetic links between Proto-Indo-European and the hypothetical Nuclear Nostratic macrofamily.

title: Five Waves Of Indo European Expansion
description: Proposes a five-wave migration model for Indo-European linguistic and cultural dispersal, integrating archaeological and genetic timelines.

title: Converging Views On Asian Prehistory
description: Synthesizes linguistic, genetic, and archaeological perspectives on Asian prehistory, highlighting convergent evidence for population movements.

title: Aryans And The Indus Civilization
description: Examines archaeological and textual evidence for cultural continuity between Indus Valley settlements and early Vedic societies.

title: Females In Rig Veda
description: Philological study of female figures, goddesses, and social roles in the Rig Veda, analyzing gender representation in early Vedic society.

title: Continuity Of European Languages
description: Linguistic analysis tracing substrate continuity in European languages, evaluating pre-Indo-European influences on modern linguistic structures.

title: Archaeological and Anthropological Studies on the Harappan Cemetery of Rakhigarhi
description: Comprehensive bioarchaeological report on Rakhigarhi burials, revealing Harappan health, diet, and population structure through skeletal analysis.

title: A Dravido Harappan Connection The Issue
description: Methodological critique of proposed Dravidian-Indus links, evaluating linguistic, archaeological, and epigraphic evidence for cultural continuity.

title: Indian Historical Writing
description: Analyzes Indian historical writing traditions, focusing on Vaṃśāvalīs and their relationship to Vedic oral transmission and chronicle composition.

title: Fabricating Evidence In Support Of The AIT
description: Critical examination of archaeological and linguistic claims used to support the Aryan Invasion Theory, addressing methodological biases.

title: Complex Genetic Origin of Indian Populations and Its Implications
description: Genomic study revealing complex admixture patterns in Indian populations, highlighting ancient migrations, endogamy, and regional genetic diversity.

title: Archaeobotany Of Ganga Plain 2500 BC
description: Paleobotanical analysis of crop remains in the Ganga Plain around 2500 BC, reconstructing early agricultural practices and environmental adaptations.

title: Scratching my head! — Criticism of Narsimhan VM et al 2019
description: Independent critique of Narasimhan et al. 2019 ancient DNA study, addressing sampling limitations and alternative interpretations of Steppe ancestry.

title: Chronology Of Kings Puranic Kings and Rigvedic Rshis
description: Cross-references Puranic king lists and Rigvedic rishi genealogies with archaeological phases of the Sindhu-Sarasvati civilization.

title: Aryas, Dasas and Dasyus
description: Analyzes Rigvedic terminology for Aryas, Dasas, and Dasyus, reinterpreting socio-cultural conflicts as intra-tribal rather than ethnic invasions.

title: Aryan Story vs True Aryan History
description: Contrasts mainstream Aryan migration narratives with textual and archaeological evidence supporting indigenous Vedic cultural development in India.

title: Varsagira Battle
description: Historical and geographical analysis of the Varsagira battle in the Rig Veda, mapping tribal movements and early Vedic geopolitical shifts.

title: Rakhigarhi and After
description: Critical response to Rakhigarhi ancient DNA findings, evaluating implications for Aryan migration theories and indigenous population continuity.

title: Out of India Case in Full
description: Comprehensive synthesis of linguistic, archaeological, and textual arguments supporting the Out of India theory for Indo-European origins.

title: The Indo European Language Family
description: Authoritative overview of Indo-European linguistic families, tracing phonological evolution, grammatical structures, and historical dispersal patterns.

title: Ahoi Aṣtamī – How Hindu Dharma Teaches Deep Ecology through Festivals
description: A festival most Hindus have forgotten exists: Ahoi Aṣṭamī as a window into how Hindu civilization transmits ecological and relational wisdom through the devotional practice of mothers, one week before Diwali.

title: AK Saran
description: A rare Indian sociologist who refused to think inside British intellectual frameworks — his is one of the few attempts to develop a genuinely Hindu social science, reading the tradition from within rather than against Western categories.

title: Alexander Solzhenitsyn
description: The man who survived the Soviet gulag and wrote its testimony: Solzhenitsyn's warning that Western atheist materialism produces the same totalitarian logic as communism remains unrefuted by anyone who has read him carefully.

title: Art and Meaning Making
description: Hindu aesthetics begins where Western aesthetics ends — the Indian tradition was interested in the effect of art on the soul, not the representation of reality, and this difference reveals two fundamentally incompatible metaphysical starting points.

title: Arthur Koestler
description: A Communist turned anti-Communist who witnessed both fascism and Stalinism firsthand — Koestler's intellectual trajectory maps the 20th century's ideological catastrophes and anticipates the civilizational questions that follow when every utopia collapses.

title: Artificial Intelligence is Here - Part 1, from Before to Now
description: AI arrives not as neutral technology but as a civilizational event: a comparison of simulation theory, Vedānta, and modern physics asks whether the intelligence emerging from our machines is something the Dhārmika tradition already has a vocabulary for.

title: Artificial Intelligence is Here - Part 2, from Now to Henceforth
description: Part 2: a proactive Dhārmika framework for navigating artificial intelligence — neither uncritical adoption nor reflexive rejection, but a principled engagement grounded in the Indian understanding of consciousness and purpose.

title: The Artist in Art
description: What happens when Western art criticism is turned inside out and evaluated through Hindu aesthetics? This project reverses the colonial critical gaze — applying Hindu aesthetic frameworks as the dominant lens to examine Western art's evolution, theory, and unspoken assumptions.

title: The Battle for Dharma in North-East
description: What has large-scale conversion and demographic change done to the Dhārmika civilizational fabric of Northeast India? A deep policy study of how missionary activity and political isolation have severed the region from the rest of Bhārata — and what reversal requires.

title: The Battle Against Hindus in Manipur
description: The Manipur conflict is not a tribal dispute but a civilizational one: how the Christian missionary conversion of Kuki tribes created a political wedge that the Indian state misreads as ethnic tension while Meitei Hindu identity is the actual target.

title: What does 'Being Indian' Mean?
description: What does Indian identity mean in light of Partition, the Muslim question, and conflicting nationalist visions from Gandhi to Savarkar to Nehru?

title: Bernd Heinrich
description: A naturalist-scientist who lives in a self-cultivated forest and finds philosophical principle in ravens and bees — Heinrich bridges the split between scientific observation and the kind of meaning-making that Hindu tradition never had to artificially construct.

title: Bhāratīya Wanderlust - A Defence of the Out-of-India Model (OIT), Part 1
description: Part 1 of a three-part dismantling of the Aryan Invasion/Migration Theory: the textual, genetic, and archaeological evidence increasingly supports an Out-of-India model for Indo-European dispersal — and the implications are civilizationally significant.

title: Bhāratīya Wanderlust - A Defence of the Out-of-India Model (OIT), Part 2
description: Part 2: examining the textual evidence for the Out-of-India model — what the Ṛgveda and comparative philology actually show when read without the Invasion Theory's assumptions baked in from the start.

title: Bhāratīya Wanderlust - A Defence of the Out-of-India Model (OIT), Part 3
description: Part 3: archaeological and genetic evidence for Out-of-India, concluding with a positive reconstruction of what the actual Āryan dispersal looked like and what it means for India's understanding of its civilizational origins.

title: Bodha, the Pyramid of Priorities and the Nature of Hindu Organizations
description: Why Hindu organizational fragmentation is a feature, not a failure: Bodha's argument that Sanatana Dharma requires a diversity of independent organizations rather than monolithic unity, and what this means for civilizational strategy.

title: Brahmacāriṇī | Navadurgā - Part 2
description: Second in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Brahmacāriṇī Devī — the form of Durgā who represents austerity, renunciation, and the discipline through which the soul approaches the absolute.

title: Candraghaṇṭā | Navadurgā - Part 3
description: Third in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Candraghaṇṭā Devī — the warrior form whose crescent-bell emblem signals the transition from inner austerity to active, world-facing śakti.

title: Chaṭh - a Living Tradition and Cultural Homecoming
description: Chaṭh as lived civilizational memory: a personal account of returning to Bihar for the festival, and what the survival of this demanding, water-centered rite reveals about the deep roots of Hindu devotional practice in the body and the land.

title: Civilizations as Kārmika Streams
description: Civilizations are not collections of events but kārmika streams — living ontological entities shaped by accumulated collective action across deep time. An essay that reframes civilizational history as metaphysics.

title: School of Conscilience
description: This school holds that the West's divorce between sciences and humanities is a civilizational pathology — and that the Indian knowledge tradition, which never separated the two, offers a working model of their reintegration.

title: What is the Core of Sanatana Dharma?
description: What is the unchanging core of Sanatana Dharma — an idea, an institution, a worldview, or a value system? Bodha's most foundational research question.

title: School of Cultural Traditionalists
description: The oldest living school in India: scholars who see themselves as contemporary interpreters of the same tradition that produced the Upanishads and the Nāṭyaśāstra — treating metaphysics, aesthetics, and philosophy as one continuous project rather than separate departments.

title: Culture and Warfare - Finding Balance Through Dharma
description: Drawing on H. G. Wells, the Mahābhārata, and dharmic philosophy to argue that the collapse of the distinction between culture and barbarism is not just a social crisis but a civilizational one — and that dharma is the only framework adequate to it.

title: Dangs - Anatomy of Religious Terror
description: What does the full anatomy of forced conversion look like in an Indian tribal district — and who bears the cost? A ground-level documentation of missionary operations in Dangs, Gujarat, tracing the cultural and social consequences for the Adivasi communities most affected.

title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 1
description: Part 1 of a three-part reconstruction of the Dāśarājña (Battle of Ten Kings) as the earliest datable event in Indian history, reexamining Ṛgveda Maṇḍala 7 using Out-of-India chronological frameworks.

title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 2
description: Part 2: a close reading of the textual data in Ṛgveda Maṇḍala 7, parsing the battle's participants, geography, and historical context against established chronologies of Indian lineages.

title: Dāśarājña Recontextualized - Part 3
description: Part 3: the concluding reconstruction of the Dāśarājña — placing the battle in specific historical and geographic context and drawing out its implications for India's deep civilizational memory.

title: Decolonization - A Personal Footprint and Some Ramp Ways
description: Decolonization as daily practice: a personal account of what it actually looks like to replace a colonial operating system of the mind — starting with the words you use, the foods you eat, the festivals you observe.

title: Decolonizing Language
description: How colonialism froze the evolution of North Indian vernaculars by installing FATE languages (Farsi, Arabic, Turkish, English) as prestige languages — and what it would take to let them grow again.

title: Dharampal School of Svayambodha
description: Built on Dharampal's archival discoveries about pre-colonial India, this school argues that Svayambodha — self-knowledge grounded in India's actual historical capacities — is the precondition for any viable Indian future, not a romantic luxury.

title: Dharampal
description: The historian who went back to British colonial archives and proved that India had thriving educational and industrial systems before colonization destroyed them — his work is the empirical foundation for any serious claim about what India actually lost.

title: Dharmic Design
description: What would architecture and design look like if grounded in Dhārmika principles of sacred geometry and Hindu aesthetics rather than Western modernism? A purva paksha of the Western design tradition, constructing an alternative framework from within the Hindu aesthetic worldview.

title: A Dharmika Frame for Sustainability and Technology
description: The Samudra Manthana as an ancient framework for thinking about technology, resource extraction, and sustainable cooperation — a Dhārmika alternative to both extractivist capitalism and romantic environmentalism.

title: The Dhārmika Gene
description: A fundamental re-articulation of Dharma: not as rule or duty but as a civilizational inheritance — the genetic-level encoding that tells Bhāratīya culture how to understand itself, its cosmos, and its obligations across generations.

title: The Difference Between Us - on One-life vs. Multiple-life Metaphysics
description: What actually separates the Hindu and Abrahamic worldviews at the deepest level is not ritual or scripture but the one-life versus multiple-life metaphysical foundation — and the implications of that single difference ramify through everything.

title: EO Wilson
description: The founding sociobiologist whose work on eusociality, biodiversity, and genetic altruism carries the closest Western scientific approximation to the Hindu understanding that human beings are embedded in a larger order — not above it.

title: Caturasūtra - Four Aphorisms
description: Four foundational aphorisms for understanding Indian civilizational consciousness, drawn from across the tradition — compact enough to internalize, substantial enough to orient an entire intellectual project.

title: Fractal Maṇḍala 1- the Macrohistoric Case for India's Civilizational Primacy
description: Part 1: the Fractal Maṇḍala as a model of Indian civilizational consciousness — multi-level, coherent, self-similar at every scale — and why recovering this model is the prerequisite for any meaningful cultural or institutional regeneration.

title: Fractal Maṇḍala 2- the Macrohistoric Case for India's Civilizational Primacy
description: Part 2: completing the Fractal Maṇḍala framework and its implications for how Hindu civilization organizes knowledge, culture, institution, and cosmos into a single coherent ontological structure.

title: Freedom of Expression
description: Why clarity about the nature of prophetic monotheism's claim on speech is a prerequisite for any meaningful Hindu engagement with the politics of free expression — the conceptual tools for a discourse that currently lacks them.

title: Recollection: The Glory of Śrī Kṛṣṇa Janmāṣṭamī
description: A pilgrimage to Mathurā, the city of Kṛṣṇa's birth: how a journey through the geography of the Kṛṣṇa legend becomes a direct encounter with the living presence of the divine in place, available to anyone who knows how to look.

title: Grand History, Part 1
description: A speculative but evidence-grounded attempt at macro-history: tracing the deep trajectory of human consciousness and civilization from pre-linguistic origins through the emergence of the great civilizational streams.

title: Hindu Frameworks of Education
description: What living models of traditional Hindu education still survive in India — and what do they demonstrate about the alternative to Western schooling? Field studies of surviving gurukulas, pathshalas, and vidyalayas documenting how Indian Knowledge Systems are transmitted in practice today.

title: What Hindu Institution Will Generate All Others?
description: Which single Hindu institution — the Hindu state, the gurukula, or the temple — could regenerate all others if everything else were lost? A question of civilizational triage and generative capacity.

title: Hindu Models of Temple Management
description: How did the traditional Hindu temple function as a self-governing civilizational institution — and what would it take to recover that from state-imposed bureaucratic management? Research building the case for culture-sensitive temple governance by documenting indigenous models of administration.

title: What Idea Must Die, for Hindu Renaissance?
description: What idea must die for Hindu renaissance to happen — caste, secularism, modernity, sarva-dharma-samabhāva, or the very category of 'religion' applied to dharma?

title: Hindu Response to Enlightenment
description: Where does Enlightenment rationalism reveal its own metaphysical blind spots — and what does Hindu philosophy offer in response? A civilizational dialogue contrasting Western secular universalism with Vedantic metaphysical plurality, proposing a Hindu diagnostic rather than a defensive rejection.

title: Is Hindu Unity Compatible With Indian Diversity?
description: Can Hindus unite without flattening the diversity that defines India? A civilizational question about whether unity and diversity are complementary or in fundamental tension.

title: Hinduism in the Blind Spot - Part 1
description: Part 1: why Hinduism sits in the blind spot of open-minded, progressive Western intellectual discourse — not from bigotry but from a structural failure of the secular liberal framework to perceive non-Abrahamic religion on its own terms.

title: Hinduism in the Blind Spot - Part 2
description: Part 2: continuing the diagnosis of why even well-meaning Western and westernized Indian intellectuals systematically fail to engage with Hinduism — and what a genuine encounter with it would actually require.

title: History is Ontic but Itihāsa is Ontologic – a Fractal Maṇḍala Essay
description: The distinction between history (ontic, event-centered) and itihāsa (ontologic, truth-centered) is not a quaint traditional category but a methodological difference with profound consequences for how India should understand and narrate its own past.

title: Indian Civilizational Consciousness
description: Written on India's 75th Independence Day: a precise exposition of what 'Indian Civilizational Consciousness' actually means — and why it is the necessary foundation for anything India does next.

title: Indic Environmentalism - A Balance between Tradition and Innovation
description: Sanātana Dharma's environmental ethic is not an add-on to its metaphysics but central to it — an argument that the dharmic relationship with nature provides a more coherent ecological framework than either Western environmentalism or techno-optimism.

title: Interchanging Dynamics of the Hindu Family
description: How has the Hindu family — long considered the basic unit of Hindu society rather than the individual — actually changed from its classical model? A field study introducing statistical and sociological rigor to questions that have been answered, until now, only by anecdote.

title: Ivan Illich
description: The sharpest institutional critic the West produced: his dissections of schooling, medicine, and economic growth argue that modern institutions systematically destroy the autonomous competence they claim to produce — a critique that hits differently once you've read Dharampal.

title: James Lovelock
description: The scientist who revived the idea that Earth is a self-regulating living system and named it Gaia — which is less a metaphor than a description of something Hindu cosmology had always held without needing a laboratory to prove it.

title: My Journey with Anveshi
description: The origin story of Anveshi, Bodha's structured temple-visit practice: how collective temple-going became a form of civilizational reconnection — and what it reveals about how living tradition actually perpetuates itself.

title: Kālarātri | Navadurgā - Part 7
description: Seventh in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Kālarātri Devī — the most fearsome form, destroyer of darkness and ignorance, whose terrifying appearance conceals absolute protection for the devotee.

title: Kapila Vatsyayana
description: Six decades of scholarship on the philosophy underlying every Indian art form — dance, architecture, painting, sculpture — Vatsyayana built the most comprehensive modern account of how Hindu arts form a unified system of knowledge, not a collection of disciplines.

title: Kātyāyanī | Navadurgā - Part 6
description: Sixth in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Kātyāyanī Devī — the fierce warrior form born to destroy the demon Mahiṣāsura, and the deity invoked for strength in the face of civilizational threat.

title: KM Munshi
description: Freedom fighter, novelist, and founder of Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan, Munshi spent his life reconstructing the cultural and historical memory that colonial education had systematically razed — the Somnath restoration was as much an act of historiography as politics.

title: Kṣetra, Śāstra, Utsava
description: On the axis connecting festival (utsava), sacred geography (kṣetra), and scripture (śāstra): how Hindu festivals are to time what temples are to space — gradients of divine access that structure both the year and the land.

title: Kūṣmāṇḍā | Navadurgā - Part 4
description: Fourth in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Kūṣmāṇḍā Devī — the form who created the universe with her smile, and who is propitiated for health, vitality, and the removal of darkness.

title: Loren Eiseley
description: An anthropologist who wrote like a poet and thought like a mystic — Eiseley's meditations on time, evolution, and consciousness are the closest Western science has come to the Hindu sense of cosmic scale and existential wonder without borrowing the vocabulary.

title: Mahāgaurī | Navadurgā - Part 8
description: Eighth in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Mahāgaurī Devī — the luminous, peaceful form that follows Kālarātri, representing the purity and grace that emerge after the destruction of all that is false.

title: Is Modernity Compatible With Hindu Values?
description: Is modernity — with its secularism, rights-framework, and assault on varṇāśrama dharma — compatible with Hindu values, or structurally hostile to Hindu civilization?

title: MS Hiriyanna
description: The definitive modern scholar of Indian philosophy: his systematic expositions of the darshanas remain the clearest English-language entry point into Hindu metaphysics and epistemology — rigorous without being reductive.

title: School of Narrative Non-fiction
description: India is a civilization that transmits knowledge through story, not proposition — this school recovers that epistemic mode and applies it to contemporary civilizational questions through long-form narrative that neither academia nor journalism can produce.

title: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
description: The philosopher of fragility who argues that modern civilization traded a robust, decentralized wisdom tradition for brittle expert systems — and that this trade is actively destroying us while we congratulate ourselves on progress.

title: Does India Need Another Republic?
description: Does India's constitution — built on rights rather than duties, and arguably biased against Hindus — need to be replaced with a second republic rooted in Dhārmika values?

title: School of Political Traditionalists
description: A school of Hindu conservatism drawing on Burke, Scruton, and Indian classical statecraft to argue for civilizational continuity over ideological reinvention — increasingly influential in post-Nehruvian policy but insufficiently read outside political circles.

title: What Do Hindus Practice, but Cannot Prove?
description: What do Hindus practice with deep śraddhā but cannot prove — karma phala siddhānta, reincarnation, mantra efficacy — and what is the right epistemic posture toward such axioms?

title: The Problem of Culture Transmission
description: Tradition is not the past preserved but a living chain of transmission — an examination of the specific mechanisms by which Hindu civilization has carried its deepest knowledge forward, and where those mechanisms are breaking today.

title: Quest for Harmony
description: Bodha's founding essay: why India's cultural redemption requires recovering its civilizational orientation toward harmony — and why this is not a sentimental project but a rigorous civilizational imperative with practical stakes.

title: Ram Swarup
description: The thinker who explained what the Vedic gods actually are, how polytheism works as a philosophical system, and why the prophetic monotheist critique of Hinduism fundamentally misunderstands what it is attacking — essential reading before any encounter with missionary argument.

title: Śrī Rāma Comes Back to Ayodhyā
description: The Ram Janmabhūmi movement as civilizational memory rather than political campaign: how the 500-year struggle for Ayodhyā maps onto the deeper Hindu understanding of sacred geography and the permanence of divine presence in place.

title: Rāma’s Journey – the Avatāra in You, a Fractal Maṇḍala Essay
description: Rāma's exile and return as inner journey: reading the Rāmāyaṇa not as history but as a map of the soul's trajectory — and why Dīpāvalī marks not just a homecoming but a cosmological reorientation of consciousness.

title: Ratha as a Bīja of Civilizational DNA, a Fractal Maṇḍala Essay
description: The ratha (chariot) as a bīja — a seed form that contains the entire Dhārmika worldview in compressed symbolic code — an essay in reading Hindu iconography as philosophical argument embedded in form.

title: राम आयेंगे!
description: A first-person account of the devotional groundswell preceding the 2024 Ayodhyā pratiṣṭhā: what a spontaneous civilizational awakening looks and feels like from the inside — and what it reveals about the living roots of Hindu consciousness.

title: Robert Ardrey
description: A playwright turned evolutionary anthropologist who argued that human territorial, hierarchical, and aggressive behavior has deep biological roots — demolishing the blank-slate assumptions that underpin liberal social engineering, decades before it became acceptable to say so.

title: Sanskṛta vs. FATE Languages
description: The battle between Saṃskṛta and FATE languages (Farsi, Arabic, Turkish, English) for the cognitive and expressive inheritance of India: why language is not a neutral medium but the primary site of civilizational contestation.

title: Śailaputrī | Navadurgā - Part 1
description: Opening the Navadurgā series: an introduction to the Navarātrī tradition followed by the theology and iconography of Śailaputrī Devī — daughter of the mountain, first of the nine forms, embodiment of primal śakti.

title: Siddhidātrī | Navadurgā - Part 9
description: Ninth and final in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Siddhidātrī Devī — the granter of all siddhis, the form who completes the Navarātrī cycle and whose worship is the culmination of the entire nine-day journey.

title: SK Ramachandra Rao
description: A Karnataka polymath whose encyclopedic work on Hindu iconography and temple architecture reveals the metaphysical logic embedded in every sculptural form and spatial arrangement — the temple not as monument but as cosmological diagram made stone.

title: Skandamātā | Navadurgā - Part 5
description: Fifth in the Navadurgā series: the theology and iconography of Skandamātā Devī — mother of Skanda (Kārttikeya), the form that holds together the energies of motherhood, courage, and cosmic order.

title: Sri Aurobindo’s School
description: Sri Aurobindo's synthesis: the spiritual evolution of consciousness as the framework within which India's civilizational mission — and humanity's future — can be understood, making it the most philosophically ambitious Hindu response to modernity.

title: Structure of Hindu Charity
description: How much charitable activity do Hindus actually conduct — and what does documenting it reveal about the false narrative that missionaries filled a philanthropic vacuum in India? Field studies measuring Hindu philanthropy in cash, kind, and services across Indian towns and villages.

title: Book Review | Svayambodha and Shatrubodha
description: A review of Pankaj Saxena's *Svayambodha and Shatrubodha*: the book that gives Hindu civilizational discourse its most precise vocabulary for self-knowledge and knowledge of adversarial forces.

title: Svayambodha and Śatrubodha - Two Wheels of Civilization
description: Hindu civilizational discourse has entered its sophisticated phase — an argument that Svayambodha (self-knowledge) and Shatrubodha (knowledge of the adversary) now constitute a canonical framework, not merely a trend.

title: Swami Vivekananda's School
description: The school that launched Hindu renaissance: Vivekananda's reading of Vedanta as both universal philosophy and basis for Hindu action produced the template every subsequent Hindu revivalist movement has worked with, consciously or not.

title: Preliminary Schema for Synaptic Reconnection to Civilizational Consciousness - Part 1
description: Part 1: a preliminary schema for reconnecting with civilizational consciousness — what it means to fill the empty space that decolonization creates with a genuinely Dhārmika ontology, epistemology, and teleology.

title: Preliminary Schema for Synaptic Reconnection to Civilizational Consciousness - Part 2
description: Part 2: completing the synaptic reconnection framework — the specific conceptual structures through which a Dhārmika consciousness can be rebuilt in a mind shaped by colonial education.

title: Temples of Madhya Pradesh
description: What do three hundred surviving ancient temple sites of Madhya Pradesh reveal about the full range of Hindu sacred architecture — and what was lost to medieval destruction? A deep architectural and sculptural study of ten major temple clusters, making the grammar of Hindu temple-building legible.

title: Terence McKenna
description: A psychedelic philosopher whose stoned-ape hypothesis, archaic revival, and novelty theory sit at the fringe of respectable discourse but ask the right questions: why did human consciousness change so dramatically, and what exactly is it moving toward?

title: Theodore Dalrymple
description: A prison doctor who documented from the inside the cultural wreckage produced by Western progressivism — his clinical observations are the evidence base for what political traditionalists argue at the level of theory.

title: Walking the Tightrope Between Big Brother and Soma
description: India walks a tightrope between two dystopias — Orwell's totalitarian surveillance state and Huxley's pleasure-addicted passivity. A Dhārmika reading of both futures and the framework that could thread between them.

title: Towards Dharma-centric Polity - Lokmanya Tilak and the Universalization of Gaṇeśa Utsava
description: As dharma recedes from both personal life and public governance, what would a genuinely dharma-centric political order look like — and is it achievable within or only beyond the current Indian constitutional framework?

title: Turners of Time - How Hindu Festivals Rotate the Year
description: Holi and Hindu festivals as turners of time: how the festival calendar structures experience, transmits ecological wisdom, and keeps alive the felt relationship between human life and cosmic rhythm across generations.

title: Voice of India School of Śatrubodha
description: The school that insists on Śatrubodha — knowing your adversary clearly — as the precondition for Hindu survival; its critique of prophetic monotheism as structurally incompatible with a pluralist civilization remains the most rigorous analysis in this space.

title: VS Naipaul
description: The Nobel laureate who traveled the colonized world and wrote what he actually saw: cultures wrecked by Islam, deformed by colonialism, and unable to recover because they've internalized their conquerors' self-image — a diagnosis as unwelcome now as when he made it.

title: Wade Davis
description: An ethnobotanist who spent decades living with indigenous peoples across the world and documented what is actually lost when a language or culture dies — not diversity as abstraction, but irreplaceable knowledge systems encoded over millennia.

title: What the Bodha Logo Means - The Square, the Circle and the Eye of Wisdom
description: The Bodha logo decoded: how a simple geometric mark encodes the entire civilizational philosophy — the fractal structure of Hindu consciousness expressed in the relationship between square, circle, and the space they share.

title: What Will Destroy Hindu Society?
description: Sita Ram Goel's 1981 warning revisited: a rigorous examination of the forces — demographic, cultural, and ideological — most likely to destroy Hindu society.

title: What Will Save Sanatana Dharma?
description: Inspired by the Śaunaka praśna of the Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad: what is the one idea, institution, or action whose presence would ensure the survival of Sanatana Dharma?

title: A Year in Hajong Village
description: How does a small Hindu tribal community in a Christian-majority region preserve its religious and cultural identity under conversion pressure? An ethnographic documentation of Hajong festivals, rituals, and livelihoods in Meghalaya's Garo Hills — a community invisible to most academic and policy discourse.

title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - I - Is Communism Compatible with Democracy?
description: Opening the Russian Revolution centenary series: the structural case that communism and democracy are incompatible — not by accident but by design, since the Leninist seizure of power required abolishing democratic legitimacy.

title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - II - How Communism Killed Freedom of Expression
description: How the Soviet suppression of free expression is not a historical curiosity but a living template — the Award Wapsi movement in India as a direct inheritance of communist tactics for manufacturing dissent.

title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - III - Communism and Treason
description: The JNU sedition scandal as a window into communism's structural relationship with treason — why the ideology, from Lenin onward, has always required betraying the nation in service of the global revolution.

title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - IV - Was Indira Gandhi a Soviet Agent?
description: The Mitrokhin Archive and what it reveals about KGB penetration of Indian politics — the evidence that Indira Gandhi's relationship with the Soviet Union went far beyond diplomatic alignment.

title: 100 Years of Russian Revolution - V - How Marxism Still Influences Indian Politics
description: Closing the Russian Revolution series: why BJP dominance has not displaced Marxism from India's institutions — the left's grip on academia, judiciary, and media persists beneath the electoral surface.

title: Ancient Temples and Modern Dacoits Part III - The Architectural Workshop of the Gurjara-Pratiharas
description: The Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty as the defining force in north Indian temple architecture — the temple clusters of Morena and Naresar as laboratories where the distinctive regional style was forged.

title: Azaan and Freedom of Expression - Are They Compatible?
description: The Sonu Nigam controversy as a test case for a structural question: whether the Islamic claim to unrestricted public religious expression can coexist with the Hindu majority's right to uninterrupted private space.

title: Case Against AOL - A Case of Malicious Prosecution
description: How Hindu spiritual organizations become targets of malicious prosecution — the Art of Living controversy as a case study in the left-missionary alliance that weaponizes courts and media against Hindu public figures.

title: Closing the Pamir Gap - The Great Game Series
description: How the Great Game played out simultaneously across multiple theaters — the closing of the Pamir Gap, Russian annexations from Manchu China, and Britain's maneuvering in East Asia all as connected moves in a single geopolitical contest.

title: Continuous, Comprehensive and Cumulative - The Knowledge Tradition of India
description: India's knowledge tradition characterized by three properties that colonial historiography denied it — its continuity across millennia, its comprehensiveness across all domains, and its cumulative rather than rupture-based development.

title: Dharmic Circuits - Sacred Pathways - Part 1
description: The Hindu geography of Bharatvarsha as a structured sacred space — how teertha yatra creates a living network of centers and pathways that defines the cultural boundaries of the civilization.

title: Dharmic Circuits - The Temple, The Grove and The Lake - Part 3
description: Sringeri as the model of a complete sacred ecology — where the temple, the forest, the river, and the Vedic institution are not separate entities but one inseparable living system.

title: Did Brahmins Monopolize Knowledge in Ancient India?
description: Dismantling the colonial claim that Brahmins monopolized knowledge in ancient India — the textual and archaeological evidence for broad-based literacy, cross-varna scholarship, and the structural openness of the gurukula tradition.

title: Does India Have a National Language?
description: The national language question as a civilizational problem, not a policy one — why Nehru's Hindi gambit failed, what the absence of a unifying language costs India, and what Sanskrit's role should be.

title: Dukh, Depression and Journeys of Life - Depression is the Absence of Dukkha - Part I
description: The provocative argument that depression is the absence of duḥkha, not its presence — how the Sanskrit framework for suffering provides an experiential map that modern psychology, focused on symptom-removal, cannot offer.

title: Equality vs Nature - How Hierarchy Can Help Us Save the Planet
description: The ecological crisis requires hierarchy, not equality — why the modern fetish of flat-horizontal social organization mirrors and accelerates the environmental destruction it claims to oppose, and what the dharmic alternative looks like.

title: Fighting for India - Britain vs Russia in the Great Game
description: The Galwan Valley clash traced back to its root cause: the Great Game's dismantling of India's traditional buffer states — why India now borders China at all is a legacy of 19th-century British-Russian competition.

title: Grasping for the Beyond - On a Critical Schism in the Modern Psyche
description: Weber's disenchantment and Jung's diagnosis of the Western mind as a madhouse of abstractions — arguing that the modern psyche's loss of the sacred is precisely the illness that India's living metaphysical tradition can address.

title: Hindu Deities in Central Asia - A Forgotten Chapter of History
description: The forgotten Indic presence deep inside Central Asia — how Hindu kingdoms and their deities extended into the Tarim Basin and toward Dunhuang before the Islamic conquests erased what had been a sustained cultural presence.

title: Hindu Liberals - The Slave Soldiers of Islamism
description: How Hindu liberals function as structural enablers of radical Islam — the Ottoman Devshirme system as a historical analogy for how dominant civilizations conscript their opponents' members to serve as their most effective weapons.

title: Hindu Samizdat - How Hindus Respond to Censorship and Bias
description: The emergence of independent Hindu digital media as a samizdat response to mainstream censorship — how the Soviet underground press becomes the analogy for what Hindu journalists built outside the left-controlled media ecosystem.

title: Hindu View of the Simulation Hypothesis
description: Nick Bostrom's simulation hypothesis examined alongside the concept of māyā — where the two frameworks converge, where they fundamentally diverge, and why the Hindu model is teleologically richer than the computational one.

title: How Lenin Tried to Foment Communist Revolution in India
description: The Second Great Game — how Lenin and the early Comintern built networks inside India to foment revolution, continuing the Russian imperial pressure on Britain's Indian possession through ideological rather than military means.

title: How Old is the Hindu Temple?
description: The antiquity of the Hindu temple examined through textual, sculptural, and archaeological evidence — dismantling the Western academic claim that temple architecture was a Buddhist invention absorbed by Hinduism.

title: How Scroll.in is Part of Omidyar's Breaking India Network
description: Scroll.in's financial architecture traced to Omidyar Network — how American philanthropic capital funds the Hinduphobic left media ecosystem in India as part of the broader Breaking India project.

title: How to Become a Superpower - Inside the Great Game
description: Central Asia's strategic logic through the lens of Gilgit-Baltistan and the Wakhan Corridor — why the Pamir corridor has always determined superpower reach and why India's loss of these buffer territories still shapes its strategic ceiling.

title: How Turkey and Germany Planned Holy Jihad Against India
description: The WWI German-Ottoman plan to use Islamic Jihad to destabilize British India via Persia and Afghanistan — how Enver Pasha and Berlin tried to turn India's Muslim population into a weapon of war.

title: In Memoriam - Life and Works of Shri N.S. Rajaram
description: Obituary and intellectual portrait of N.S. Rajaram — his place in the Voice of India tradition, his contributions to Vedic chronology and the Out of India theory, and what his death means for the Hindu Renaissance.

title: In the Name of Defending Islam - I - Understanding the Muslim Psyche
description: The 2020 Delhi Riots as a diagnostic event — what they reveal about the structural dynamics of the radical Muslim psyche, the myth of Hindu provocation, and the pattern that has repeated across fourteen centuries of Islamic expansion.

title: In the Name of Defending Islam - II - How Radical Muslims Play Victim
description: How radical Islam systematically inverts the aggressor-victim relationship — the global media architecture that transforms Hindu self-defense into Hindu aggression, and why this inversion is structurally necessary to the ideology.

title: Intellectual Terrorism of the Left - The Case for Dr. Makarand Paranjape
description: The cancellation of Makarand Paranjape at Carleton University — how left academic networks suppress Hindu intellectual voices not through argument but through institutional intimidation, and what it reveals about who controls Western universities.

title: Kaiser, Sultan and the Holy Jihad in India
description: The Second Great Game — how Germany and Ottoman Turkey built a plan around mobilizing Islamic Jihad inside India during WWI, making the Kaiser and Sultan strange allies in a scheme that came closer to success than most histories acknowledge.

title: Letter to a Moderate Muslim
description: Closing the Unfinished Agendas series: the open letter that argues the moderate Muslim does not exist — that moderation and radical Islamic ideology are structurally incompatible, and the secular Hindu's belief in this figure is a lethal self-deception.

title: Making of Early Kashmir - The Making of a New Literary Star
description: Review of Shonaleeka Kaul's The Making of Early Kashmir — how the Rajatarangini constructs Kashmiri identity through sacred landscape, and why this emic reading of history challenges the colonial separation of geography from meaning.

title: The Man Who Turned Down the Skull Cap - How India Sees Narendra Modi
description: What Modi's refusal of the skull cap tells us about who he is — reading his symbolic politics not as Hindu chauvinism but as the first coherent expression of a civilizational self-respect that India's post-colonial elite had systematically suppressed.

title: The Many Meanings of the Hindu Temple
description: The Hindu temple as simultaneously sacred mountain, cosmic axis, human body, and gateway between worlds — a layered symbolic reading of the temple form as encoded theological knowledge made visible in stone.

title: On What is a Hindu Temple
description: Dr. R. Nagaswamy on the essential nature of the Hindu temple — what it actually is, why its origins predate Buddhism, and how the Western academic genealogy that credits Buddhism with inventing the temple form gets the history exactly backward.

title: Pundits - How Hindu Hillmen Secretly Mapped Central Asia
description: How trained Hindu hill-men — the Pundits — secretly mapped the unmappable territories of Central Asia for British India's Great Game intelligence operations, disguised as pilgrims and traders, measuring distances by prayer bead.

title: Puranas as the Source for a Chronology of Indian History
description: A Purāṇic chronological framework for ancient Indian history — building on Pargiter's genealogical reconstruction and Subhash Kak's Mahābhārata dating to propose a coherent timeline for the previous six manvantaras.

title: Race for Lhasa - The Great Game Series
description: The race to Tibet at the turn of the 20th century — how Britain and Russia competed for influence over Lhasa after the Pamir Gap was closed, and what the Younghusband Expedition's forced entry into Tibet meant for the final phase of the Great Game.

title: Re-imposing the Jizyah - Decoding Sharjeel Imam's Speech
description: Sharjeel Imam's Shaheen Bagh speech decoded through classical Islamic jurisprudence — the argument that modern India's treatment of Hindus already constitutes an informal Jizyah, and that what Imam demanded was simply its open declaration.

title: Shri Ram Swarup on the Communist Challenge
description: Ram Swarup's early writings on communism as a spiritual and civilizational threat — recovering a neglected dimension of the Voice of India thinker whose work on Islam and Christianity overshadowed his equally penetrating critique of Marxism.

title: Story of a Priest
description: Portrait of the head priest of the Hoysala-era Veera Narayana Temple at Belavadi — how a 12th-century heritage temple survives and what the priest's life reveals about the community of practice that keeps the living tradition alive.

title: Tapan Ghosh - A True Hindu Warrior
description: Obituary for Tapan Ghosh — the Hindu Samhati founder who gave decades of his life to documenting and resisting the persecution of Hindus in Bengal, and whose death represents an irreplaceable loss to the Bengali Hindu community.

title: Teaching and Research - The Rot That Has Set In
description: The HRD Ministry's proposal to de-emphasize research in college promotions — why the policy response is inadequate to the depth of the rot, and what the real diagnosis of India's academic collapse requires.

title: Thou Shalt Not Comment - The Dictatorship of Scroll.in
description: How Scroll.in built ideological censorship into its comment moderation — the left-liberal digital media's control of who gets to respond, and why shutting down the comments section was the tell that revealed the site's function as propaganda rather than journalism.

title: The Truth and the Humbug in Greta Thunberg's Climate Change Speech
description: Separating the genuine environmental alarm in Greta Thunberg's speech from its ideological packaging — what the climate movement gets right, what it gets wrong, and why the solution requires hierarchy and dharma rather than equality and activism.

title: Unfinished Agendas of Radical Islam - Case 1 - Lebanon
description: Lebanon as the case study of how a Muslim minority achieves demographic dominance — tracing the mechanisms by which a Christian-majority country was transformed, and what this trajectory means for every country with a growing Muslim population.

title: Unfinished Agendas of Radical Islam - Case 2 - Armenia and Turkey
description: The Armenian Genocide as the first genocide of the 20th century — how Turkey's secular Muslim state proved no less lethal to its Christian minorities than an explicitly Islamic one, and why the denial continues to this day.

title: Unfinished Agendas of Radical Islam - Case 3 - Greece and Spain
description: Greece and Spain as the cases of temporarily reversed Islamic conquest — why the population transfers that ended Ottoman rule in Greece are cited by Hindu nationalists as the model India failed to follow, and what Spain's Reconquista reveals about civilizational recovery.

title: Unfinished Agendas of Radical Islam - The Global Gameplan
description: Opening the Unfinished Agendas series: the ideological architecture of radical Islam's global strategy — how the anti-CAA riots are not a spontaneous reaction but a calculated move in a long-running civilizational campaign.

title: We the Hindus - What the CAB Means for Hindu Civilization
description: The Citizenship Amendment Act read as a civilizational turning point — not refugee policy but India's first post-colonial assertion of its Hindu identity, and what the civilizational stakes of this assertion actually are.

title: Why Liberals Hate Baahubali
description: Why India's left-liberal cultural establishment hated Baahubali — the film as a mass rejection of the self-hating colonial aesthetic and a reassertion of Hindu heroism, beauty, and civilizational confidence that the establishment had worked decades to suppress.

title: Why Saba Naqvi is Afraid of Mullahs on TV - And Why We Must Encourage That
description: The internal contradiction in secular Muslim commentators opposing mullah visibility on TV — what Saba Naqvi's discomfort reveals about who actually speaks for Indian Muslims, and why Hindu society should want the authentic voice to be heard.

title: Witzel's Realm - On Reputationist Concerns Over India's Reclamation of Its History
description: A direct response to Michael Witzel's attack on Indian historical revisionism — defending the legitimacy of indigenous Purāṇic chronology against Western academic gatekeeping dressed up as methodological rigor.

description: Brahminic knowledge, ritual, and spiritual practice.

description: Mind, intellect, perception, aesthetic experience, and knowledge.

description: Time, seasons, cosmic cycles, and the ordering of history.

description: Land, soil, geography, and the natural ground of civilization.

description: Human life, society, relations, and the categories of personhood.

title: Kṣatriya — The Warrior
description: War, governance, kingship, and the political order.

description: Trade, agriculture, livestock, and the productive order.

title: Śūdra — Craft and Service
description: Artisans, service vocations, and the material arts.

description: Drama, dance, music, and the performing arts.

title: Śabdādi — Sound and Language
description: Speech, sound, grammar, and the philosophy of language.

title: Vanauṣadhi — Forest and Medicine
description: Plants, herbs, forests, and Āyurvedic knowledge.

title: Siṃhādi — Animals
description: Animals, birds, and the natural world.

title: Vāri — Water
description: Rivers, oceans, rain, and aquatic life.

title: Śaila — Mountains
description: Mountains, rocks, and elevated geography.

title: Pura — The City
description: Settlements, architecture, urban life, and built space.

title: Vyoma — The Sky
description: Sky, atmosphere, celestial bodies, and the heavens above.

title: Dig — Direction and Space
description: The eight directions and the spatial ordering of the cosmos.

title: Naraka — The Infernal
description: Hell realms, punishment, and the consequences of karma.

title: Pātāla — The Underworld
description: Nāgas, the underworld, and subterranean cosmology.

title: Nānārtha — Polysemy
description: Words with multiple meanings — the semantic richness of Sanskrit.

title: Saṅkīrṇa — The Mixed
description: Miscellaneous categories not covered by other vargas.

title: Avyaya — Indeclinables
description: Particles, indeclinables, and the grammatical skeleton of Sanskrit.

title: Viśeṣyanighna — Adjectives
description: Qualifiers, attributes, and the adjectival vocabulary of Sanskrit.
